x_posts.text: She came in on a late withdrawal at No. 89. She beat Keys 6-1 in the first set. Now Yuliia Starodubtseva is in the Charleston final. One win from her first WTA title. 🎾 #TennisFanrecap #CharlestonOpen #WTA

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text: Pegula vs. Starodubtseva — Charleston final day is here. Top seed holding off the run of the week. WTA 500 and 280 ranking points on the line. Who wins? #TennisFanrecap #CharlestonOpen #WTA

facebook.long_form:
```
There's a reason everyone was paying attention to Charleston this week — and her name is Yuliia Starodubtseva.

The Ukrainian entered the Credit One Charleston Open on a last-minute wildcard spot, ranked No. 89 in the world. Most players in that position just hope to win a round or two and leave with some ranking points. Starodubtseva had other plans.

She powered through the draw, and in the semifinals she faced Madison Keys — one of the biggest servers on the WTA Tour. The result was stunning: 6-1, 6-4. Starodubtseva broke Keys eight times and was clinical from start to finish in just 73 minutes.

Standing between her and the title is No. 5-seeded Jessica Pegula, who survived Iva Jovic in three sets to get here. Pegula has the experience and consistency to be a tough out in any clay final.

But this week belongs to Starodubtseva. Can she finish it? Drop your prediction below.
```

#### Image Caption -- 3:00 PM ET

```
From No. 89 to the Charleston final. Yuliia Starodubtseva is one win from making history. Will she pull it off against Pegula?
```

---

article.headline: Charleston Final: Starodubtseva's Cinderella Run Meets Pegula in WTA 500 Showdown

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text: Marco Trungelliti is 36 years old. He has never won an ATP title. Today he plays his first-ever ATP final in Marrakech — the oldest debut finalist in Open Era history. What a story. #TennisFanrecap #Marrakech #ATP

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text: The Marrakech final: 36-year-old Trungelliti vs. 19-year-old Jodar. A 17-year age gap. One is making his debut. Both in their first ATP final. Tennis is wild. #TennisFanrecap #Marrakech #ATP

facebook.long_form:
```
Tennis has a new Open Era record — and the man setting it is 36 years old.

Marco Trungelliti of Argentina has played professional tennis for over a decade. He has survived Challengers, Futures, and every level of the tour that most people never hear about. Today, in Marrakech, he played his first-ever ATP Tour final at the age of 36.

That makes him the oldest player in Open Era history to debut in an ATP final, surpassing Victor Estrella Burgos who was 34 when he reached his first final in 2015.

Waiting for him? Rafael Jodar of Spain — 19 years old.

The 36-vs-19 storyline writes itself. One player who has been grinding for decades finally arrived at the biggest moment of his career. The other just got out of his teenage years and is playing like he belongs.

This is why we watch tennis.

Which generation do you have winning in Marrakech?
```

#### Image Caption -- 10:30 AM ET

```
At 36, Marco Trungelliti played his first-ever ATP Tour final today in Marrakech. The oldest debut finalist in Open Era history. Against a 19-year-old. Tennis doesn't always follow the script.
```

---

article.headline: At 36, Trungelliti Becomes the Oldest First-Time Finalist in Open Era History at Marrakech

stories.title: Bucharest Final — Navone vs. Merida Aguilar (Tier 2)

x_posts.label: Post 3-1
x_posts.text: Navone saved TWO match points in a 3-hour, 32-minute semifinal to reach the Bucharest final. His opponent: a qualifier who had zero tour wins before this week. #TennisFanrecap #TiriacOpen #ATP

x_posts.label: Post 3-2
x_posts.text: Daniel Merida Aguilar arrived in Bucharest as a qualifier with zero tour-level wins. He is in the final. He could win today. Qualifier magic is alive in 2026. #TennisFanrecap #TiriacOpen #ATP

facebook.long_form:
```
The Tiriac Open final in Bucharest today has everything that makes tennis compelling.

On one side: Mariano Navone of Argentina, a seasoned competitor who had to dig deeper than anyone expected just to get here. In his semifinal against Botic van de Zandschulp, Navone trailed, faced two match points, and came back to win in 3 hours and 32 minutes. That kind of fight doesn't come from talent alone.

On the other side: Daniel Merida Aguilar of Spain, 21 years old, a qualifier. He arrived in Bucharest this week without a single ATP Tour-level match win to his name. He is now in the final.

Two first-time finalists. One title. One story gets a happy ending today.
```

#### Image Caption -- 12:30 PM ET

```
Navone survived two match points in a 3-hour semifinal. Merida Aguilar arrived as a qualifier without an ATP win. Today they meet in the Bucharest final. Tennis always finds a way.
```

---

article.headline: Bucharest Final: Navone Saved Match Points to Get Here. Now He Faces a Qualifier Who Had Nothing to Lose

stories.title: Monte Carlo Masters — No. 1 Race Begins (Tier 1)

x_posts.label: Post 4-1
x_posts.text: Monte Carlo draw is set. Alcaraz defends 1,000 points as champion. Sinner is ~190 points back at No. 2 and has zero to defend. The clay season No. 1 race starts Monday. #TennisFanrecap #MonteCarlo #ATP

x_posts.label: Post 4-2
x_posts.text: Sinner won Indian Wells. Then Miami. Without dropping a set. First man in history to complete the Sunshine Double without losing a set. Now Monte Carlo. The No. 1 race is on. #TennisFanrecap #MonteCarlo #ATP

facebook.long_form:
```
The clay season has officially arrived — and with it, the biggest ranking question in men's tennis.

The Rolex Monte-Carlo Masters draw is set. Carlos Alcaraz, the defending champion, enters as No. 1 with 13,590 ranking points. Jannik Sinner enters at No. 2, roughly 190 points behind.

Here's what makes this week so loaded: Alcaraz is defending every point from his 2025 Monte Carlo title — all 1,000 of them. Sinner missed the event last year and has zero points to defend.

Sinner also just made history, winning both Indian Wells and Miami without dropping a single set. First man ever to complete the Sunshine Double without a set loss.

The path to No. 1 runs through Monte Carlo. Nine top players withdrew — including Djokovic, Fritz, Draper, Shelton, and Rune — opening the draw wide. The race starts Monday.

Who do you have going deep this week?
```

#### Image Caption -- 8:00 AM ET

```
Monte Carlo week is here. Alcaraz defends 1,000 points. Sinner defends zero. The No. 1 ranking race has officially begun on clay. Who do you have?
```

---

article.headline: Monte Carlo 2026: The No. 1 Race Between Alcaraz and Sinner Officially Begins on Clay

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text: Clay season injury check: Navarro dealing with health issues — 4-9 in 2026. Fritz knee tendinitis — may need extended rest. Rune targeting Madrid return after Achilles surgery. Tough stretch for three big names. #TennisFanrecap #ATP #WTA

facebook.long_form:
```
As the clay season gets underway, three of tennis's most prominent names are watching from the sidelines.

Emma Navarro, who reached a career-high ranking of No. 8 last season, has withdrawn from both Charleston and Miami citing ongoing health struggles. Her 2026 record is 4-9, and her ranking has dropped to No. 27. She said: "I have been dealing with some health struggles over the past year or so that I'm continuing to sort out."

Taylor Fritz, the American No. 7, skipped Monte Carlo with knee tendinitis that has persisted for more than six months. He's hinted that an extended break may be necessary to fully heal.

Holger Rune ruptured his left Achilles tendon at Stockholm last September. Now ranked No. 29, he's targeting a return at the Madrid Open in mid-May. There's cautious optimism, but an Achilles recovery is never a guarantee.

Three different stories. One common thread: the clay season is arriving without some of the names fans were hoping to see.
```

#### Image Caption -- 6:00 PM ET

```
Three players, three health challenges heading into clay season. Navarro, Fritz, and Rune are all fighting to get back — but the timeline for each is uncertain.
```

article.headline: Clay Season Begins Without Them: The Health Struggles of Navarro, Fritz, and Rune
